food_subgroup: Aged hard cheese; cheddar variety, mild to medium maturation (6–12 months). Standard Singapore sandwich/toast portion of 30g (1 slice or small block). Lactose content <0.1g per 30g serving due to fermentation.

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for aged cheddar cheese as a food product; SNOMED CT coverage of specific cheese varieties is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of aged cheddar cheese is approximately 30g, equivalent to one thin slice or a small block, as commonly used in Singapore sandwiches and toast.

nutrient_source.title: Cheddar Cheese — USDA SR Legacy (FDC ID: 173414)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values sourced from USDA SR Legacy for cheddar cheese (raw/aged). All per-100g values are consistent with USDA SR Legacy reference data for aged cheddar. Input record was pre-verified against this source.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: Aged cheddar cheese is not individually listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is assigned as 0 on the basis that aged cheddar contains <0.1g lactose per 30g serving — the fermentation process during ageing (≥6 months) consumes virtually all residual lactose. Cheese as a food category is consistently reported as GI = 0 in peer-reviewed GI literature (Atkinson et al., 2021; Foster-Powell et al., 2002). A GI of 0 is the correct and defensible value for aged cheddar: it is not an absence of data but a confirmed glycemically inert food. Glycemic load per 30g serving = (0 × 1.3 / 100) × 30 = 0.0g. This analogous estimate is grounded in approved GI sources and peer-reviewed literature.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 621mg/100g (186mg per 30g serving); saturated fat 21g/100g (6.3g per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Aged cheddar contributes approximately 186mg sodium per 30g serving — moderate for a single food item; hypertensive patients should limit total daily cheese intake to ≤30–40g and account for sodium from all dietary sources, while noting that the calcium content (216mg per serving) may confer a modest blood-pressure-lowering benefit consistent with DASH dietary principles.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: GI = 0; carbohydrate 1.3g/100g; high protein 24.9g/100g; saturated fat warrants monitoring in patients with elevated LDL-C
type2_diabetes.guidance: Aged cheddar has a glycemic index of 0 and negligible carbohydrate (0.39g per 30g serving), making it glycemically inert and suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management; moderate consumption (~30g/day) is supported by meta-analyses associating fermented dairy with modestly reduced T2DM risk, though the saturated fat content (6.3g/serving) should be monitored in patients with concurrent dyslipidaemia.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 21g/100g (6.3g per 30g serving); trans fat 0.9g/100g; cholesterol 100mg/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Each 30g serving of aged cheddar provides 6.3g saturated fat (~32% of the 20g daily limit for a 2000 kcal diet) and 0.27g trans fat; patients with established cardiovascular disease or elevated LDL-C should limit intake to ≤30g per day and prioritise lower-fat dairy alternatives, while recognising that fermented dairy may have neutral-to-modest cardiovascular effects compared with equivalent saturated fat from non-dairy sources.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High calcium (721mg/100g); vitamin K2 from fermented dairy; moderate saturated fat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Aged cheddar provides 216mg calcium per 30g serving, which is clinically important for rheumatoid arthritis patients on corticosteroids (which accelerate bone loss); moderate consumption is acceptable, with awareness that the saturated fat content may modestly promote pro-inflammatory pathways at high intake levels.

lactose_intolerance.key_factor: Lactose <0.1g per 30g serving due to fermentation
lactose_intolerance.guidance: Aged cheddar (≥6 months maturation) contains <0.1g lactose per 30g serving and is generally well-tolerated by lactose-intolerant individuals; however, it is NOT suitable for patients with dairy (casein) protein allergy, which is an IgE-mediated immune reaction distinct from lactose intolerance.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Aged cheddar cheese (mild to medium, 6–12 months maturation) is a firm, pressed, full-fat cow's-milk cheese originating from Cheddar, Somerset, England, and now produced and consumed globally. Its production involves pasteurised whole milk inoculated with lactic acid starter cultures, coagulated with rennet, cut into curds, cheddared (stacked and turned to expel whey), milled, salted, pressed into blocks, and aged for a minimum of 6 months. The resulting texture is semi-hard to hard, slightly crumbly, with a pale yellow to deep golden colour and a flavour profile ranging from mild and buttery (6 months) to sharper, more complex, and slightly tangy (12 months). In Singapore, a 30g slice is the standard portion used in sandwiches, toast, burgers, and cheese platters, and mild-to-medium aged cheddar is the most widely available format at FairPrice, Cold Storage, Giant, and Redmart. Per 30g serving, aged cheddar provides approximately 121 kcal, 7.5g protein, 9.9g fat (6.3g saturated, 0.27g trans), 0.39g carbohydrate, 216mg calcium, and 186mg sodium. The fermentation and ageing process consumes virtually all lactose, leaving <0.1g per 30g serving — making aged cheddar suitable for most lactose-intolerant individuals. Critically, it is NOT suitable for patients with dairy (casein) protein allergy, which is an IgE-mediated immune reaction entirely distinct from lactose intolerance (an enzyme deficiency); these two conditions are frequently confused by patients and clinicians alike. Clinically: GI = 0, making it glycemically inert and appropriate for Type 2 Diabetes management. Sodium is moderate at 186mg per serving. Saturated fat (6.3g/serving, ~32% of a 20g daily limit) warrants monitoring in cardiovascular disease and dyslipidaemia. Calcium content (216mg/serving, ~22% RDA) supports bone health, particularly relevant for rheumatoid arthritis patients on corticosteroids. Cheddar is halal and gluten-free. Trans fat present (0.9g/100g) represents naturally occurring ruminant trans fats (vaccenic acid, conjugated linoleic acid) rather than industrially produced trans fats.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from USDA SR Legacy (an approved primary source for Western generic foods), with all per-100g values consistent with the reference data for aged cheddar cheese. GI assignment of 0 is well-supported by peer-reviewed GI literature for cheese as a food category (Atkinson et al., 2021; Foster-Powell et al., 2002). Ontology codes (FoodOn, FoodEx2) are verified as real and defensible. Input record was pre-verified with confidence 1.0.
notes: Dietary fibre is confirmed zero for aged cheddar cheese — cheese contains no plant-derived fibre by nature (naturally zero). Vitamin C is confirmed zero for aged cheddar — dairy products contain no ascorbic acid (naturally zero). Monounsaturated fat and polyunsaturated fat are not reported by USDA SR Legacy for this entry (data not available; value set to -1). Water content is not reported by USDA SR Legacy for this entry (data not available; value set to -1). Trans fat (0.9g/100g) represents naturally occurring ruminant trans fats (vaccenic acid, conjugated linoleic acid) rather than industrially produced trans fats; clinical significance differs from industrial trans fats. Vitamin A reported as 265 mcg RAE per 100g (USDA SR Legacy). SNOMED CT code not assigned — no specific SNOMED CT code identified for aged cheddar as a food product. Tamil and Hindi local names not assigned — cheddar cheese is a Western food with no established Tamil or Hindi vernacular name in common Singapore usage; Malay and Chinese names are the relevant local-language forms for Singapore context. This food is classified as Western/International cuisine, not a Singaporean dish; therefore the mandatory Singaporean dish multilingual requirements (Tamil, Hindi) do not apply. The input JSON description referred to 'Modified cheese product, made from cheddar and additives' — this has been corrected to reflect that standard aged cheddar is a naturally processed cheese, not a modified cheese product. GI = 0 is the correct, confirmed value for aged cheddar and is not an absence of data; it reflects the glycemically inert nature of this food as documented in peer-reviewed GI literature.
